post.title: Shifting to U.S. Energy: An Economic Suicide Pact for India!!
post.excerpt: The global energy map is being redrawn. In this chaos, a dangerous narrative is emerging in New Delhi’s policy circles: the idea that India should pivot its energy imports toward the United States to "secure" its supply. Oil and gas imports from the U.S has...
post.body:
The global energy map is being redrawn. In this chaos, a dangerous narrative is emerging in New Delhi’s policy circles: the idea that India should pivot its energy imports toward the United States to "secure" its supply. Oil and gas imports from the U.S has already creeping up. Let’s be brutally honest: for an economy like India, trading the Middle East and Russia for the U.S. isn’t diversification—it is a Strategic Tax that will bleed the foreign exchange reserves and cripple the industrial competitiveness. Here is the hard-hitting reality of why the "Long-Haul" energy model is a disaster in the making. 1. The Geography of Bankruptcy: The "Freight Multiplier" The first law of energy economics is proximity. A tanker from the Persian Gulf takes roughly a week to hit the Indian coast. In contrast, a voyage from the U.S. Gulf Coast is a month and half odyssey. In the high-risk world of 2026, this five week difference is a financial death sentence. The freight cost for U.S. crude isn’t just higher—it is exponentially prohibitive. We are effectively paying a $5 per barrel "Distance Tax" before the oil even touches a refinery. When you multiply that by millions of barrels, you aren't just buying oil; you are subsidizing the global shipping industry with Indian taxpayer money. 2. The Speculative Blind Spot: The 35-Day "Floating Risk" In a market where prices can swing by $20 in a week due to a single tweet or a drone strike, time is a liability. When an Indian refiner buys U.S. oil, they are forced into a 35-day speculative trap. If we buy at a peak price today, and the market cools while that ship is still crossing the Atlantic, the importer is "underwater" for weeks. This massive lag prevents Indian refiners from being

"agile." We are locking in yesterday’s high prices for tomorrow’s consumption. This "capital at sea" doesn’t just hurt oil companies; it widens our Current Account Deficit (CAD) and puts unnecessary, structural pressure on the Rupee. 3. The Technical Mismatch: Refining for a Diesel Economy This is the most critical factor that often gets ignored. India is a Diesel-First economy. Our trucks, our farms, and our industries run on the middle of the barrel. Russian Urals and Arab Medium crudes are "Medium-Sour" grades—they are the perfect chemical fit for Indian refineries, yielding a high percentage of high-quality diesel. U.S. crude, primarily from the Permian Basin, is "Light-Sweet." It is "clean," yes, but it yields a massive surplus of Naphtha and Petrol—products we already have in abundance or don't need in the same volumes. By shifting to U.S. crude, we are forcing our world-class refineries to work against their design. The Bottom Line: Don't Trade Security for Efficiency Shifting to the U.S. isn't just about changing a phone number on a contract; it's about accepting a higher cost of living for 1.4 billion people. https://www.linkedin.com/posts/know-your-commodity_shifting-to-us-energy-an-economic- suicide-activity-7438538281635536896-D3hx
post.author: Know Your Commodity
